title: Elasticity of Demand
statement:
Se o preço de um bem aumenta de R\$10 para R\$12 e a quantidade demandada
    cai de 100 para 80 unidades, qual é a elasticidade-preço da demanda
    (em valor absoluto)?
solution:
Usando a fórmula da elasticidade ponto:
    \[
      \varepsilon = \left|\frac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta P / P}\right|
                  = \left|\frac{-20/100}{+2/10}\right|
                  = \left|\frac{-0{,}2}{0{,}2}\right| = 1{,}0
    \]
    Portanto, a demanda é unitariamente elástica. Alternativa \textbf{(B)}.

title: Fiscal Multiplier
statement:
Suppose a closed economy has a marginal propensity to consume (MPC) of 0.8
    and the government increases its spending by \$500 million.

    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
      \item Calculate the fiscal multiplier and the total change in GDP.
      \item Explain how the presence of income taxes would affect your answer.
    \end{enumerate}
solution:
\textbf{(a)} The fiscal multiplier in a closed economy without taxes is:
    \[
      k = \frac{1}{1 - \text{MPC}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8} = 5
    \]
    Total change in GDP:
    \[
      \Delta Y = k \cdot \Delta G = 5 \times \$500\text{M} = \$2{,}500\text{ million}
    \]

    \textbf{(b)} With proportional income taxes at rate $t$, the multiplier becomes:
    \[
      k = \frac{1}{1 - \text{MPC}(1-t)}
    \]
    Since $\text{MPC}(1-t) < \text{MPC}$, the denominator increases and the
    multiplier falls. Taxes act as an \emph{automatic stabilizer}, dampening
    both expansions and contractions.

title: Some Rubber Duckies
statement:
The toy store ``Duck Duckies'' sells rubber duckies. The supply of rubber
    duckies is given by the inverse function $P = \dfrac{Q_s - 20}{3}$, where
    $P$ is the price in reais and $Q_s$ is the quantity supplied. The
    equilibrium price is R\$~16 and the equilibrium quantity is 68 rubber
    duckies.

    Which of the following could be the true demand curve for this market?
solution:
At equilibrium, the demand curve must satisfy $P^* = 16$ and $Q^* = 68$.
    Check each option by substituting $Q_d = 68$:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item (A) $P = 50 - 0.5 \times 68 = 50 - 34 = \mathbf{16}$ \checkmark
      \item (B) $P = 45 - 0.4 \times 68 = 17.8$ \texttimes
      \item (C) $P = 60 - 0.8 \times 68 = 5.6$ \texttimes
      \item (D) $P = 40 - 0.3 \times 68 = 19.6$ \texttimes
      \item (E) $P = 55 - 0.6 \times 68 = 14.2$ \texttimes
    \end{itemize}
    Only option \textbf{(A)} passes through the equilibrium point $(68, 16)$.

choices:
The result depends on the complementarity or substitutability of the
          foreign workers with American workers. If foreign workers are
          complements, the changes will decrease the welfare of the U.S
          workforce. If they are substitutes, the changes will increase the
          welfare of the U.S workforce.

The changes will increase the welfare of the U.S workforce as more
          jobs will be available for American citizens, leading to lower
          competition for jobs and consequently higher wages.

The changes will decrease the welfare of the U.S workforce as it
          will attract more skilled workers from abroad, due to the possibility
          of earning more than \$100,000, leading to higher competition for
          jobs and consequently lower wages.

The changes will have no effect on the welfare of the U.S workforce
          as the number of H1B visas issued is relatively small compared to
          the total workforce, leading to an irrelevant impact on job
          availability and wages.

The changes will increase the welfare of the U.S workforce, as new
          jobs will open only for American citizens, but the overall effect on
          wages will be negligible due to the high demand for skilled labor
          in the U.S.
solution:
The key insight is that the effect of restricting skilled foreign labor
    depends on whether those workers are \emph{complements} or
    \emph{substitutes} to American workers.

    \begin{itemize}
      \item \textbf{Substitutes:} Foreign workers compete directly with
            Americans for the same jobs. Restricting them reduces labor supply
            in that segment, raising wages for Americans~$\Rightarrow$
            welfare \textbf{increases}.
      \item \textbf{Complements:} Foreign skilled workers boost American
            workers' productivity (e.g.\ a foreign software engineer enables
            more American project managers). Restricting them reduces
            productivity and wages for Americans~$\Rightarrow$ welfare
            \textbf{decreases}.
    \end{itemize}

    Since the question cannot be answered without knowing the relationship,
    \textbf{(A)} is the only correct alternative.

title: Classicus Met Keynesson
statement:
Consider the following 2020 news report:

    \begin{center}
      \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{../images/q0005_fig1.jpeg}
    \end{center}

    The irony presented by the news is the opposition between the liberal
    model and the Keynesian model for solving economic crises.

    During the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, the economies of several countries
    slowed down sharply. Suppose that two economists had been hired by the
    Brazilian government at that time to design mechanisms to stimulate the
    country's economy: Classicus, who follows the classical economic tradition,
    and Keynesson, who follows the Keynesian tradition. Knowing that both aim
    to rebalance the level of goods and services produced in the country (GDP),
    that is, to achieve the level of full employment, mark the correct
    alternative.
choices:
Keynesson proposes an increase in government spending to stimulate
          the economy through aggregate demand and, in this way, raise the
          level of output.

For Classicus, the solution to the crisis is an increase in the
          money supply, which will lead to a fall in the interest rate and a
          consequent rise in aggregate demand, stimulating the economy and
          increasing output.

For Keynesson, the only possible solution to the crisis would be a
          reduction in the country's tax burden, which would increase
          households' disposable income and stimulate consumption and output;
          other mechanisms, such as higher government spending, would have no
          effect.

Both Classicus and Keynesson agree that an increase in government
          spending raises aggregate demand and, consequently, output, although
          they disagree about the magnitude of the resulting inflationary
          effect.

For Keynesson, an increase in coronavirus stimulus packages raises
          government spending, which consequently increases aggregate supply
          and rekindles the economy, increasing output.
solution:
The Keynesian model argues that in a recession the economy can be stuck
    below full employment because of insufficient aggregate demand. The
    prescription is \textbf{fiscal stimulus}: an increase in government
    spending shifts $AD$ rightward, raising output and employment.

    Errors in the other options:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item \textbf{(B)} An increase in money supply is the
            \emph{monetarist/classical} prescription, not Classicus
            (classical economists rely on market self-correction, not
            activist money supply policy).
      \item \textbf{(C)} Keynesson accepts multiple tools; government
            spending is his primary lever.
      \item \textbf{(D)} Classical economists do \emph{not} agree that
            government spending raises output (crowding-out, Say's Law).
      \item \textbf{(E)} Government spending affects aggregate
            \emph{demand}, not supply.
    \end{itemize}

    Answer: \textbf{(A)}.

title: The Good Liar
choices: (Up, Left).
(Up, Right).
(Down, Left).
(Down, Right).
There is no Nash Equilibrium in this game.

title: Excuse Me, I Was Here First!
statement:
In the loanable funds market, suppose the supply and demand for loanable
    funds are given by:
    \[
      S(r) = 400 + 10r \qquad I(r) = 700 - 20r
    \]
    where $r$ is the real interest rate (in percentage points) and $I$ is
    private investment (in billions of dollars). Initially, government
    borrowing is zero ($G = 0$), and the economy is at full employment. The
    government decides to increase spending by \$150 billion, financing this
    entirely through borrowing ($G = 150$).

    By how much does private investment change due to the crowding-out effect?
choices: Private investment increases by \$150 billion.
Private investment increases by \$100 billion.
Private investment remains unchanged.
Private investment decreases by \$100 billion.
Private investment decreases by \$150 billion.
solution:
\textbf{Step 1 — Initial equilibrium} ($G=0$):
    \[ 400 + 10r = 700 - 20r \implies 30r = 300 \implies r^* = 10\% \]
    \[ I_0 = 700 - 20(10) = 500 \text{ billion} \]

    \textbf{Step 2 — After government borrowing} ($G=150$):
    Total demand for loanable funds $= I(r) + G = (700-20r) + 150 = 850-20r$.
    \[ 400 + 10r = 850 - 20r \implies 30r = 450 \implies r^{**} = 15\% \]
    \[ I_1 = 700 - 20(15) = 400 \text{ billion} \]

    \textbf{Change in private investment:}
    \[ \Delta I = 400 - 500 = -\$100 \text{ billion} \]

    Private investment \textbf{decreases by \$100 billion}. Note that full
    crowding-out would require $\Delta I = -\$150B$; here it is only
    partial because the supply of loanable funds is upward-sloping.
    Answer: \textbf{(D)}.

title: No Traces Left Behind
statement:
In the nation of Fermatland, inflation follows an unconventional model.
    After the adoption of a new currency, a product originally priced at $p$
    in year~1 has value $p^t$ in year~$t$. There are 100 companies offering
    the same product, with company~$i$ having an initial price of~$i$
    (for $1 \leq i \leq 100$).

    Each year, the government provides a subsidy exactly equal to the price of
    one randomly selected company's product for that year. The subsidy must
    be spent entirely within the year and cannot be saved.

    You wonder whether it is ever possible to buy \emph{two} products in a
    single year using the subsidy exactly --- that is, with no money left over.

    How many years can this happen?

    \textit{Hint:} Fermat's Last Theorem states that there are no positive
    integer solutions to the equation $a^n + b^n = c^n$ for $n \geq 3$.
choices: Forever

title: The Great Bread Divide
statement:
On planet OBECON, there are two closed economies with no government
    intervention: Econoland and Obeland. Each economy functions efficiently,
    producing only one final good---bread---and both have many machines and
    workers devoted to increasing production. The size of the consumer market
    in both economies is also the same.

    In both Econoland and Obeland, capital and labor contribute to production
    in the same proportion, and both economies have the same total amount of
    each factor available. Based on this information, which factor explains
    why Econoland has a higher Gross Domestic Product?
choices: The demand for bread is greater in Econoland than in Obeland.

Econoland experiences constant returns to scale, while Obeland
          experiences increasing returns to scale.
Capital is used more efficiently in Obeland than in Econoland.

The level of technology and innovation in Econoland is higher than
          in Obeland.
Workers are less committed in Econoland than in Obeland.
solution:
Using the Solow growth framework, output is given by
    $Y = A \cdot F(K, L)$, where $A$ is \textbf{Total Factor Productivity
    (TFP)} (technology). Since both economies have:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item The same quantities of $K$ and $L$,
      \item The same factor shares,
      \item The same market size (so demand is not the driver),
    \end{itemize}
    the only remaining explanation for Econoland's higher GDP is a
    \textbf{higher level of technology and innovation} ($A_\text{Econ} >
    A_\text{Obe}$).

    Errors: (A) demand drives prices, not supply-side output in this model;
    (B) IRS in Obeland would \emph{help} Obeland; (C) contradicts the
    premise; (E) less-committed workers would reduce Econoland's output.
    Answer: \textbf{(D)}.

title: Beyond the IRR-ational Choice
choices: Project~A should be chosen, regardless of the cost of capital.
Project~B should be chosen, regardless of the cost of capital.
Both projects have an Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of 10\%.
Both projects have an Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of 20\%.

The choice depends on the cost of capital; Project~A is better if
          the rate is above a 10\% rate.
solution:
Reading the NPV profile graph:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Both curves cross the horizontal axis (NPV~$= 0$) at
            $r = 20\%$~$\Rightarrow$ \textbf{both projects have IRR~$= 20\%$}.
      \item The two curves intersect at $r = 10\%$ (the \emph{crossover
            rate} or Fisher intersection).
      \item For $r < 10\%$: Project~A has higher NPV~$\Rightarrow$ choose A.
      \item For $10\% < r < 20\%$: Project~B has higher NPV~$\Rightarrow$
            choose B.
      \item For $r > 20\%$: both NPVs are negative.
    \end{itemize}

    Option (E) is wrong because A is better \emph{below} 10\%, not above.
    Options (A) and (B) are wrong because the best choice depends on $r$.
    Option (C) is wrong: the IRR is 20\%, not 10\%.

    Answer: \textbf{(D)}.

title: Phillips, We Have a Problem
subtopic: History of Economic Thought / Phillips Curve
statement:
1929 and the 1970s were worrisome economic periods for those alive at the
    time, marked respectively by the Great Depression and two oil shocks. In
    both periods, stagnant economic growth was a problem, and unemployment
    rose to sky-high levels.

    Another important factor to consider when looking at an economic crisis is
    inflation. Given your previous knowledge, how was inflation during such
    periods?
choices:
Prices experienced major deflation in both crises, which decreased
          purchasing power and discouraged spending.

In the Great Depression, prices surged before decreasing rapidly
          due to expansionary monetary policy. In the 1970s, on the other
          hand, prices stayed low for the entire period, except for the rise
          in oil prices.

The Great Depression was a deflationary crisis, while the 1970s saw
          high inflation levels in a phenomenon known as stagflation.

In the Great Depression and in the 1970s, prices suffered
          deflationary pressure, in accordance with the Phillips Curve
          relationship between unemployment and inflation.

Prices surged in both crises, which pushed Central Banks around the
          world to implement contractionary monetary policy.
solution:
\begin{itemize}
      \item \textbf{Great Depression (1929):} A severe demand collapse led
            to \textbf{deflation} (prices fell sharply). Unemployment reached
            $\sim$25\% in the US.
      \item \textbf{1970s oil shocks:} Supply-side shocks (OPEC embargo 1973,
            Iranian revolution 1979) pushed costs up, causing simultaneously
            high inflation \emph{and} high unemployment --- a phenomenon
            called \textbf{stagflation}, which broke the traditional
            Phillips Curve trade-off.
    \end{itemize}

    Option (D) is wrong precisely because the 1970s \emph{violated} the
    Phillips Curve: high unemployment coincided with high inflation, not
    deflation.

    Answer: \textbf{(C)}.

title: Doubling the Target
statement:
Consider an infinite number of firms, each producing an identical good.
    Firm~$i$ has a marginal cost of production equal to $2^i$, for all
    $i \in \mathbb{Z}$. There are no fixed costs. The market demand is given
    by the function $Q = 2^{-P}$. A technological innovation is introduced
    that allows every firm to halve its marginal cost. Assuming firms operate
    under Cournot competition, how much will the joint profit of all the
    companies increase after the new technology?
solution:
Halving every firm's MC is equivalent to replacing $2^i$ with
    $2^{i-1} = 2^{(i-1)}$, i.e.\ shifting the index by $-1$. Since $i$
    ranges over all integers $\mathbb{Z}$, the set of marginal costs
    $\{2^i : i \in \mathbb{Z}\}$ is \emph{identical} before and after the
    shift (the set is unchanged; only the labeling shifts).

    Because the market structure --- the distribution of MCs across the
    infinite set of firms and the demand curve --- is identical before and
    after, the Cournot equilibrium (prices, quantities, profits) is
    \textbf{unchanged}.

    With infinitely many Cournot competitors whose MCs span $(0, \infty)$,
    the market approaches perfect competition with $P \to 0$, $\pi \to 0$
    regardless. The innovation leaves joint profit at 0 both before and after.

    Change in joint profit: $\mathbf{0\%}$. Answer: \textbf{(B)}.

title: Two Intersections
statement:
Consider two investment options:
    \begin{enumerate}
      \item An investment that offers \textbf{simple interest} at rate $r$:
            $A_s = P(1 + rt)$
      \item An investment that offers \textbf{compound interest} at rate $r$:
            $A_c = P(1+r)^t$
    \end{enumerate}
    where $A$ is the accumulated amount, $P$ is the principal, $r$ is the
    annual rate (decimal), and $t$ is the time in years.

    Carlos invests \$1,000 in the compound option; Frederico invests \$1,000
    in the simple option. What can we assume about their decisions?
choices:
Carlos made the optimal choice, as compound interest always yields
          higher returns than simple interest.

Frederico made the optimal choice, as simple interest is more
          predictable and less risky than compound interest.

Both Carlos and Frederico may have made the optimal choice,
          depending on the investment duration.

Both will receive the same amount over the long term, as the
          difference between simple and compound interest diminishes over time.
Both options will yield the same return after $\dfrac{P}{r}$ years.
solution:
Compare $A_s = P(1+rt)$ and $A_c = P(1+r)^t$:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item At $t = 0$: both equal $P$.
      \item At $t = 1$: $A_s = P(1+r) = A_c$. \textbf{Equal.}
      \item For $0 < t < 1$: $(1+r)^t < 1 + rt$ by concavity of power
            function~$\Rightarrow$ \textbf{simple interest is higher}.
            (Frederico does better for short-term investments.)
      \item For $t > 1$: $(1+r)^t > 1 + rt$~$\Rightarrow$
            \textbf{compound interest is higher}.
            (Carlos does better for long-term investments.)
    \end{itemize}

    Therefore, neither choice is universally optimal; it depends on the
    investment duration. Both may have made the correct decision.
    Answer: \textbf{(C)}.

title: Double It and Give It to the Next Person
statement:
A popular TikTok influencer runs a social experiment called ``Double it
    and give it to the next person.'' The rules are simple: a participant is
    offered \$1 and can either keep it or double it and pass it to the next
    person, who then gets offered \$2. That next person can either keep the
    \$2 or also double it for the following round. The game continues as long
    as participants keep passing.

    Everyone shares the same utility function for this game,
    $u(x, y) = x + \sqrt{200y}$, where $x$ is the participant's own payoff
    and $y$ is the next participant's payoff.

    How many rounds will the game last before a participant decides to keep
    the money instead of passing it?
choices: 1 round.
3 rounds.
5 rounds.
7 rounds.
9 rounds.
solution:
At round $n$, the person holds $A_n = 2^{n-1}$ dollars.
    \begin{itemize}
      \item \textbf{Keep:} $x = 2^{n-1}$, $y = 0$~$\Rightarrow$
            $u = 2^{n-1} + 0 = 2^{n-1}$.
      \item \textbf{Pass:} $x = 0$, $y = 2^n$~$\Rightarrow$
            $u = 0 + \sqrt{200 \cdot 2^n}$.
    \end{itemize}

    The person passes if $\sqrt{200 \cdot 2^n} > 2^{n-1}$. Squaring both
    sides:
    \[
      200 \cdot 2^n > 2^{2n-2} \implies 200 > 2^{n-2}
    \]

    Check by round:
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccc}
      \toprule
      Round $n$ & $2^{n-2}$ & Pass? \\
      \midrule
      1  & $0.5$  & Yes \\
      2  & $1$    & Yes \\
      $\vdots$ & $\vdots$ & Yes \\
      9  & $128$  & Yes ($200 > 128$) \\
      10 & $256$  & No  ($200 < 256$) \\
      \bottomrule
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}

    Participants pass in rounds 1--9 and the 10th person keeps. The game
    lasts \textbf{9 rounds} before someone keeps.
    Answer: \textbf{(E)}.

title: Comparing Betas
statement:
The founders of ObeCorp and ObeconCo started with one idea in the same
    garage. But after disagreeing over who should be CEO, they split up and
    started being competitors in the same industry. You, as a prospective
    investor, are analyzing both companies.

    You already know that the risk-free rate is 3\%, and the expected return
    on the market is 9\%. Additionally, your intern gives you the information
    that ObeCorp has a $\beta$ (systematic risk of assets) of 1.5, while
    ObeconCo has a $\beta$ of 0.8.

    Assuming the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) holds, what can be said
    about the expected return for each stock?
choices:
Both companies have the same expected return of 13.5\% since they
          operate in the same industry.

ObeCorp has an expected return of 12\% while ObeconCo has an
          expected return of 8.8\%.

ObeCorp has an expected return of 12\%, because it carries greater
          systematic risk, while ObeconCo has an expected return of 7.8\%.

ObeCorp has an expected return of 13.5\% while ObeconCo has an
          expected return of 7.8\%.

Since the risk-free rate is more relevant than the beta, ObeCorp
          has an expected return of 12\% and ObeconCo of 7\%.
solution:
CAPM formula: $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot (E(R_m) - R_f)$

    Market risk premium: $E(R_m) - R_f = 9\% - 3\% = 6\%$

    \begin{itemize}
      \item \textbf{ObeCorp} ($\beta = 1.5$):
            $E(R) = 3\% + 1.5 \times 6\% = 3\% + 9\% = \mathbf{12\%}$
      \item \textbf{ObeconCo} ($\beta = 0.8$):
            $E(R) = 3\% + 0.8 \times 6\% = 3\% + 4.8\% = \mathbf{7.8\%}$
    \end{itemize}

    ObeCorp's higher $\beta$ means it carries greater systematic risk, which
    demands a higher expected return. They operate in the same industry but
    have different risk profiles. Same industry does \emph{not} imply same
    expected return.
    Answer: \textbf{(C)}.

title: To Be or Not to Be (Independent)
statement:
The image below shows reports that President Trump once considered firing
    Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell over disagreements about monetary
    policy.

    \begin{center}
      \includegraphics[width=0.78\textwidth]{../images/q0016_fig1.png}
    \end{center}

    Suppose the economy has a natural unemployment rate of 5\% and that the
    short-run Phillips curve is given by:
    \[
      \pi = \pi^e - 0.5\,(u - u_N)
    \]
    where $\pi$ is the actual inflation, $\pi^e$ is the expected inflation,
    $u$ is the unemployment rate and $u_N$ is the natural rate. At first the
    economy has $u = 3\%$ and $\pi^e = 2\%$. After the announcement,
    $\pi^e$ rises to 4\%. Suppose the Fed maintains its current policy stance
    (unemployment unchanged at 3\% in the short run). What are the
    implications for inflation and unemployment in the short run and long run?
choices:
In the short run, inflation increases to 5\% while unemployment
          remains at 3\%; in the long run, unemployment returns to 5\% and
          inflation stabilizes at 4\%.

In the short run, inflation decreases to 5\% while unemployment
          remains at 3\%; in the long run, unemployment returns to 5\% and
          inflation stabilizes at 4\%.

In the short run, inflation increases to 5\% while unemployment
          remains at 3\%; in the long run, unemployment returns to 5\% and
          inflation stabilizes at 3\%.

In the short run, inflation decreases to 5\% while unemployment
          remains at 3\%; in the long run, unemployment returns to 3\% and
          inflation stabilizes at 3\%.

In the short run, inflation remains at 3\% while unemployment
          increases to 5\%; in the long run, unemployment returns to 3\% and
          inflation stabilizes at 4\%.
solution:
\textbf{Short run} (Fed keeps $u = 3\%$, $\pi^e$ jumps to 4\%):
    \[
      \pi = 4 - 0.5\,(3 - 5) = 4 + 1 = \mathbf{5\%}
    \]
    Inflation rises from 3\% to 5\%; unemployment stays at 3\%.

    \textbf{Long run} (expectations adjust; economy returns to $u_N$):
    In the long run $u \to u_N = 5\%$ and $\pi = \pi^e$ (no surprises).
    Since the Fed does not tighten policy to fight the higher expectations,
    inflation expectations settle at 4\%, so:
    \[
      \pi = 4 - 0.5\,(5 - 5) = \mathbf{4\%}
    \]
    The long-run Phillips curve is vertical at $u = 5\%$ with
    $\pi = \pi^e = 4\%$.
    Answer: \textbf{(A)}.

title: Cushioning the Fall
statement:
Consider an economy with a progressive income tax system:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item 10\% on income up to \$50,000
      \item 25\% on income between \$50,000 and \$100,000
      \item 40\% on income above \$100,000
    \end{itemize}
    The economy has 1,000 individuals: 500 earning \$80,000 and 500 earning
    \$120,000. During a recession, everyone's income falls by \$40,000.

    What is the tax revenue as a share of total income before and after?
solution:
\textbf{Before recession:}
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Earner at \$80,000: tax $= 0.10 \times 50{,}000 + 0.25 \times
            30{,}000 = 5{,}000 + 7{,}500 = \$12{,}500$
      \item Earner at \$120,000: tax $= 0.10 \times 50{,}000 + 0.25 \times
            50{,}000 + 0.40 \times 20{,}000 = 5{,}000 + 12{,}500 + 8{,}000
            = \$25{,}500$
    \end{itemize}
    Total income $= 500 \times 80{,}000 + 500 \times 120{,}000 =
    \$100{,}000{,}000$\\
    Total tax $= 500 \times 12{,}500 + 500 \times 25{,}500 =
    \$19{,}000{,}000$\\
    Tax share $= 19{,}000{,}000 / 100{,}000{,}000 = \mathbf{19.00\%}$

    \textbf{After recession} (incomes fall by \$40,000):
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Earner at \$40,000: tax $= 0.10 \times 40{,}000 = \$4{,}000$
      \item Earner at \$80,000: tax $= 0.10 \times 50{,}000 + 0.25 \times
            30{,}000 = \$12{,}500$
    \end{itemize}
    Total income $= 500 \times 40{,}000 + 500 \times 80{,}000 =
    \$60{,}000{,}000$\\
    Total tax $= 500 \times 4{,}000 + 500 \times 12{,}500 = \$8{,}250{,}000$\\
    Tax share $= 8{,}250{,}000 / 60{,}000{,}000 = \mathbf{13.75\%}$

    The progressive system automatically reduces the tax burden in a
    recession (automatic stabilizer). Answer: \textbf{(E)}.

title: Going with the Flow
statement:
In a labor market, let $f$ be the \emph{job-finding rate} (probability an
    unemployed person finds a job) and $s$ be the \emph{job separation rate}
    (probability an employed person loses their job). Both rates are constant.

    What is the steady-state unemployment rate?
choices: $u^* = \dfrac{f}{f+s}$
$u^* = \dfrac{s}{s+f}$
$u^* = \dfrac{s-f}{s+f}$
$u^* = s \cdot f$
$u^* = \dfrac{1}{s+f}$

title: Selic Under Fire
statement:
During the third term of President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, the Central
    Bank of Brazil has been heavily criticized for maintaining the Selic rate
    at high levels even amid slowing inflation. Regarding the Central Bank,
    choose the correct alternative:
choices:
The President of the Central Bank of Brazil is Fernando Haddad,
          appointed by the President of the Republic and approved by the
          Senate to serve a four-year term, which does not coincide with the
          presidential term.

Like the Federal Reserve, the Brazilian Central Bank's main
          objective is to keep inflation within a target set by the National
          Monetary Council and unemployment at the level of full employment.

One of the recent advances in the management of the Brazilian
          Central Bank's activities was the establishment of its autonomy.
          According to economic theory, this measure helps increase the
          institution's credibility and, consequently, reduces the cost (in
          terms of output and unemployment) of fighting inflation.

The Central Bank is responsible in Brazil for implementing fiscal
          policy, which includes setting taxation targets, managing public
          investments, and choosing the interest rate.

New Keynesian economists often represent the Central Bank's reaction
          function to inflation and the output gap through the Phillips Curve.
solution:
\begin{itemize}
      \item \textbf{(A) Wrong:} The president of the BCB is Gabriel
            Galípolo (not Fernando Haddad, who is Finance Minister).
            The structure description is partially correct but the name
            is wrong.
      \item \textbf{(B) Wrong:} The BCB has a \emph{single mandate}
            (price stability / inflation target set by the CMN). The Fed
            has a \emph{dual mandate} (inflation + full employment).
      \item \textbf{(C) Correct:} Central bank autonomy (established
            in Brazil in 2021) increases credibility by reducing political
            interference. With more credible anti-inflation commitment,
            the disinflationary sacrifice ratio (output/employment lost
            per unit of inflation reduction) decreases.
      \item \textbf{(D) Wrong:} The BCB implements \emph{monetary} policy,
            not fiscal policy. Fiscal policy is the Treasury/Finance
            Ministry's domain.
      \item \textbf{(E) Wrong:} New Keynesians represent the CB reaction
            function through the \emph{Taylor Rule}, not the Phillips Curve.
    \end{itemize}
    Answer: \textbf{(C)}.

title: Don't Lose Interest
statement:
Fernando is a risk-averse investor evaluating two 10-year government bonds
    with identical face values and yields: Bond~A (coupon-paying) and Bond~B
    (zero-coupon). Duration approximates interest rate sensitivity:
    \[
      \text{Duration} \approx -\frac{1}{P} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta y}
    \]
    A higher duration means greater price sensitivity to rate changes.

    If Fernando wants to minimize interest rate risk, which bond should he
    choose?
choices:
Bond~A, even though coupon-paying bonds have higher duration than
          zero-coupon bonds of the same maturity.

Bond~A, because coupon-paying bonds have lower duration than
          zero-coupon bonds of the same maturity.

Bond~B, even though zero-coupon bonds have higher duration than
          coupon-paying bonds of the same maturity.

Bond~B, because zero-coupon bonds have lower duration than
          coupon-paying bonds of the same maturity.

Both bonds have identical interest rate risk since they have the
          same maturity.
solution:
\textbf{Key fact:} For bonds of the same maturity, a coupon-paying bond
    has \emph{lower} Macaulay duration than a zero-coupon bond.

    \textbf{Why?} Duration is the weighted average time to receive cash flows.
    A coupon bond pays cash throughout its life (earlier coupons pull the
    weighted average down), while a zero-coupon bond pays everything at
    maturity. Therefore:
    \[
      D_\text{coupon} < D_\text{zero} = \text{maturity} = 10 \text{ years}
    \]

    Fernando wants to \emph{minimize} duration~$\Rightarrow$ choose the bond
    with \textbf{lower duration}~$\Rightarrow$ \textbf{Bond~A} (coupon-paying).

    Option (E) is wrong: same maturity $\neq$ same duration.
    Answer: \textbf{(B)}.

title: The Expected Cellphone
statement:
Suppose the cellphone market in Obeconland has new phones (sellers' min
    price R\$~1,000; buyers' WTP R\$~1,200) and used phones (sellers' min
    R\$~500; buyers' WTP R\$~700). Buyers cannot distinguish types before
    purchase. The proportion of new phones is $\pi$ ($0 \leq \pi \leq 1$),
    known to buyers. Trades are determined by bargaining. Mark the correct
    alternative.
choices: If $\pi = 0$, the price of a cellphone would be $P^* = \text{R\$}~700$.

The market failure is information asymmetry and the phenomenon is
          moral hazard.

If $\pi < \frac{3}{5}$, the market only works for used cellphones,
          with $500 \leq P^* \leq 700$.
If $\pi > 0$, both new and used cellphones will be traded.

If buyers knew the type before purchase, only new cellphones would
          be traded.
solution:
Buyers' expected WTP for a random phone: $\pi \cdot 1{,}200 + (1-\pi)
    \cdot 700 = 700 + 500\pi$.

    New phones sell only if buyers' WTP $\geq$ sellers' minimum for new:
    \[
      700 + 500\pi \geq 1{,}000 \implies \pi \geq \tfrac{3}{5}
    \]

    If $\pi < \frac{3}{5}$: buyers won't pay enough for new phones
    $\Rightarrow$ new phone sellers exit $\Rightarrow$ only used phones
    trade at some $P^* \in [500, 700]$. \textbf{(C) is correct.}

    Errors:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item \textbf{(A)} If $\pi=0$, all phones are used; price is between
            500 and 700 (not necessarily exactly 700).
      \item \textbf{(B)} The phenomenon is \emph{adverse selection} (not
            moral hazard, which arises \emph{after} a transaction).
      \item \textbf{(D)} Small $\pi$ prevents new phones from trading.
      \item \textbf{(E)} With full information, both types would trade
            (there are gains from trade for both).
    \end{itemize}
    Answer: \textbf{(C)}.

title: American Dream
statement:
A family wants to purchase a house worth \$500,000. They make a 20\% down
    payment and finance the remaining amount with a 30-year fixed-rate mortgage
    at an annual interest rate of 6\%. What is the approximate required monthly
    mortgage payment to fully repay the loan in this exact time frame?

title: Robin Hood
statement:
Consider the following income distribution for a small economy with 8
    individuals:

    \vspace{6pt}
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{lc}
      \toprule
      \textbf{Individual} & \textbf{Annual Income (\$)} \\
      \midrule
      Alice   & 3,000  \\
      Bob     & 4,000  \\
      Carlos  & 5,000  \\
      Diana   & 9,000  \\
      Eva     & 21,000 \\
      Felipe  & 25,000 \\
      Gabriel & 35,000 \\
      Helena  & 64,000 \\
      \bottomrule
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \vspace{6pt}

    The government taxes the top 25\% earners \$10,000 each and distributes
    the total equally among the bottom 50\% earners. The poverty line is 50\%
    of the \emph{status quo} median income. By what percentage does this policy
    reduce the number of people in poverty?

    Median of $a_1,\ldots,a_{2n}$ is $\dfrac{a_n+a_{n+1}}{2}$. Round to
    nearest integer; if fractional part is 0.5, round to nearest even integer.
solution:
\textbf{Step 1 --- Status quo median and poverty line:}\\
    Sorted incomes: 3k, 4k, 5k, 9k, 21k, 25k, 35k, 64k ($n=4$).\\
    Median $= \frac{a_4 + a_5}{2} = \frac{9{,}000 + 21{,}000}{2} = \$15{,}000$.\\
    Poverty line $= 50\% \times 15{,}000 = \$7{,}500$.

    \textbf{Step 2 --- People in poverty before policy:}\\
    Alice (\$3k), Bob (\$4k), Carlos (\$5k): all $< \$7{,}500$. \textbf{3 people.}

    \textbf{Step 3 --- Apply policy:}\\
    Top 25\% = top 2 earners: Gabriel and Helena, each taxed \$10,000.\\
    Total transferred $= \$20{,}000$, split equally among bottom 50\% (4
    people): each receives $\$5{,}000$.\\
    New incomes:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Alice: $3{,}000 + 5{,}000 = \$8{,}000 > \$7{,}500$ \\
      \item Bob: $4{,}000 + 5{,}000 = \$9{,}000 > \$7{,}500$ \\
      \item Carlos: $5{,}000 + 5{,}000 = \$10{,}000 > \$7{,}500$ \\
      \item Diana: $9{,}000 + 5{,}000 = \$14{,}000 > \$7{,}500$
    \end{itemize}

    \textbf{Step 4 --- People in poverty after policy:} \textbf{0 people.}

    \textbf{Reduction:} $\dfrac{3 - 0}{3} \times 100 = \mathbf{100\%}$.

title: Fed Fights Foreign Forces
statement:
Consider a small open economy where $CA = 200 - 50e$ and
    $KA = 100(i - i^*)$, with $e$ the exchange rate (USD per domestic
    currency). Initially $i = 8\%$, $i^* = 4\%$. The foreign rate rises to
    $i^* = 6\%$; the central bank keeps $i = 8\%$.

    Find $e_\text{new} - e_\text{old}$ to maintain BOP equilibrium
    ($CA + KA = 0$). Round to nearest integer; if 0.5, round to even.
solution:
\textbf{BOP equilibrium condition:} $CA + KA = 0$, i.e.\
    $200 - 50e + 100(i - i^*) = 0$.

    \textbf{Before} ($i^* = 4\%$):
    \[
      200 - 50e + 100(8 - 4) = 0 \implies 600 = 50e \implies e_\text{old} = 12
    \]

    \textbf{After} ($i^* = 6\%$):
    \[
      200 - 50e + 100(8 - 6) = 0 \implies 400 = 50e \implies e_\text{new} = 8
    \]

    \[
      e_\text{new} - e_\text{old} = 8 - 12 = \mathbf{-4}
    \]

    Intuition: a higher foreign rate makes domestic assets relatively less
    attractive, reducing capital inflows ($KA$ falls). To restore BOP
    equilibrium, the domestic currency must \emph{depreciate} ($e$ falls,
    since $e$ is USD per domestic unit). Answer: $\mathbf{-4}$.

title: Maximizing Shareholder Value
statement:
A manufacturing company evaluates an M\&A acquisition costing \$100M
    upfront. Short-term CF scenarios: Base (45\%, \$80M), Bear (40\%,
    half of base), Bull (15\%, four times base). Long-term CF: \$200M
    certain. Shareholders are risk-neutral.

    Calculate the IRR per period. Give as \% rounded to nearest integer;
    if 0.5, round to even.
solution:
\textbf{Step 1 --- Expected short-term CF:}
    \[
      E[\text{CF}_1] = 0.45 \times 80 + 0.40 \times 40 + 0.15 \times 320
      = 36 + 16 + 48 = \$100\text{M}
    \]

    \textbf{Step 2 --- Set NPV = 0:}
    \[
      -100 + \frac{100}{1+r} + \frac{200}{(1+r)^2} = 0
    \]
    Let $x = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$:
    \[
      200x^2 + 100x - 100 = 0 \implies 2x^2 + x - 1 = 0
      \implies (2x - 1)(x + 1) = 0
    \]
    Taking the positive root: $x = \dfrac{1}{2}$, so $1 + r = 2$,
    i.e.\ $r = 1 = \mathbf{100\%}$ per period.

title: Matthew 14:13--21
statement:
A fractional-reserve banking system has a required reserve ratio of 20\%.
    The central bank injects \$400 million of new reserves via open market
    operations. Banks lend all excess reserves; no currency leakages.
    Households' MPC = 0.6.

    Calculate the total change in GDP (in \$ millions). Round to nearest
    integer; if 0.5, round to even.
solution:
\textbf{Step 1 --- Money multiplier:}
    \[
      m = \frac{1}{\text{reserve ratio}} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5
    \]
    New money supply $= \$400\text{M} \times 5 = \$2{,}000\text{M}$.

    \textbf{Step 2 --- Spending/fiscal multiplier:}
    \[
      k = \frac{1}{1 - \text{MPC}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5
    \]

    \textbf{Step 3 --- Total change in GDP:}
    \[
      \Delta Y = 2{,}000 \times 2.5 = \mathbf{\$5{,}000\text{ million}}
    \]

    The biblical reference (Matthew 14: feeding 5,000 with a few loaves)
    alludes to the multiplier effect ``multiplying'' the initial injection.

title: Follow the Leader
statement:
Two oil companies compete in a Stackelberg duopoly (rotating leadership)
    where the firm producing less becomes leader next period. Firm~A leads in
    period 1. Market demand: $Q = 360 - p$; both firms have $MC = 120$, no
    fixed costs; interest rate 20\%. No collusion; game is infinite-horizon.

    Find $(S_A + S_B) - (C_A + C_B)$ over 10 years, where $S_i$ is firm~$i$'s
    total output under the new rule and $C_i$ under Cournot.
solution:
\textbf{Cournot equilibrium:}\\
    Profit: $\pi_i = (240 - q_i - q_j)q_i$; FOC: $240 - 2q_i - q_j = 0$.\\
    Symmetric: $q^C = 80$ each, $Q^C = 160$.\\
    Over 10 periods: $C_A = C_B = 800$, so $C_A + C_B = 1{,}600$.

    \textbf{Stackelberg equilibrium:}\\
    Follower BR: $q_F = \dfrac{240 - q_L}{2}$.\\
    Leader: $\max (240 - q_L - q_F)q_L = \max \dfrac{240 - q_L}{2} q_L$.\\
    FOC: $q_L^S = 120$; $q_F^S = 60$.

    \textbf{Leadership rotation:}\\
    Period 1: A leads ($q_A=120$, $q_B=60$). B produces less $\Rightarrow$
    B leads next.\\
    Period 2: B leads ($q_B=120$, $q_A=60$). A produces less $\Rightarrow$
    A leads next.\\
    Pattern alternates every period.

    Over 10 periods (A leads in 1,3,5,7,9; B leads in 2,4,6,8,10):
    \[
      S_A = 5 \times 120 + 5 \times 60 = 600 + 300 = 900
    \]
    \[
      S_B = 5 \times 60 + 5 \times 120 = 300 + 600 = 900
    \]
    \[
      S_A + S_B = 1{,}800
    \]

    \textbf{Difference:}
    \[
      (S_A + S_B) - (C_A + C_B) = 1{,}800 - 1{,}600 = \mathbf{200}
    \]

    The rotating-Stackelberg policy \emph{increases} total output (closer to
    competitive level), which was the government's goal to reduce market
    power externalities.

title: The ``Beverage'' Curve
statement:
Consider the following chart regarding the labor market in the United
    States from 2000 to 2025.

    \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{../images/q0028_fig1.png}
    \end{center}

    \begin{enumerate}[(a)]
      \item (20 points) What is the relationship between unemployment and the
            job openings (vacancy) rate? Explain why this relationship exists.

      \item (20 points) As you can see, the green triangle line has only two
            dots, one in March 2020 and another in April 2020. Which dot do
            you think corresponds to March and which to April? What could
            explain such a sharp difference over this short period?

      \item (20 points) Let market tightness be defined as the ratio between
            vacancies ($v$) and unemployment ($u$), i.e.\ $\theta = v/u$.
            Based on the chart, how does market tightness in the period from
            April 2022 to August 2025 (purple rhombus line) compare to that
            from December 2000 to August 2008 (blue square line)?

      \item (20 points) What is the expected behavior of market tightness
            during a recession? And during an economic boom?

      \item (20 points) What relationship do you expect between market
            tightness and wage growth?
    \end{enumerate}
solution:
\textbf{(a)} There is a negative relationship between the unemployment
    rate and the vacancy rate. When the economy is doing well, businesses
    create more job openings to meet increased demand, lowering unemployment.
    Conversely, during downturns, businesses reduce hiring, raising
    unemployment. This downward-sloping locus is the \emph{Beveridge Curve}.

    \textbf{(b)} The dot to the left corresponds to March~2020, when
    unemployment was still low. The dot to the right (higher unemployment,
    lower vacancies) corresponds to April~2020, when COVID-19 lockdowns
    caused a sudden surge in unemployment and collapse in hiring.

    \textbf{(c)} From December~2000 to August~2008 market tightness was
    moderate and relatively stable. From April~2022 to August~2025 tightness
    increased significantly, with vacancies far exceeding unemployment,
    indicating an exceptionally tight labour market driven by strong
    post-pandemic demand.

    \textbf{(d)} During a recession, market tightness falls as unemployment
    rises and vacancies fall. During a boom, it rises as unemployment falls
    and vacancies increase.

    \textbf{(e)} There is typically a positive relationship between market
    tightness and wage growth. When $\theta$ is high, employers compete
    intensely for scarce workers and bid up wages. When $\theta$ is low,
    excess labour supply reduces wage pressure.

title: Music or Noise?
statement:
Lucas likes to play music, but noise bothers his community --- even
    though they appreciate some amount of music. Every day, he chooses a
    proportion of the day to spend playing music, keeping the rest silent.
    His utility function is
    \[
      u(s,m) = 1 - (s^2 + m^2),
    \]
    where $s \in [0,1]$ is the proportion of the day spent in silence and
    $m \in [0,1]$ is the proportion spent playing music. Because time is
    split between silence and music:
    \[
      s + m = 1.
    \]

    \begin{enumerate}[(a)]
      \item (20 points) Without any intervention, what proportion of music
            $m^*$ does Lucas choose?

      \item (30 points) The community's utility function is
            \[
              U(s,m) = 1 - (s^2 + 3m^2),
            \]
            reflecting that the community values silence more than music.
            \begin{enumerate}[(i)]
              \item Calculate the proportion of music $m_c$ the community
                    would like Lucas to play. Explain why it differs from
                    $m^*$.
              \item Is $(s_c, m_c)$ Pareto efficient? Identify \emph{all}
                    values of $m$ that constitute Pareto efficient allocations.
            \end{enumerate}

      \item (20 points) Find the socially optimal allocation $(s_s, m_s)$
            that maximises the unweighted aggregate welfare
            $W = u(s,m) + U(s,m)$.

      \item (30 points) The community decides Lucas's music proportion should
            be lower. Knowing his utility is measured in dollars, elaborate
            and describe \textbf{two mechanisms} that could induce him to
            decrease his choice of $m$ and explain how each alters his
            incentives.
    \end{enumerate}
solution:
\textbf{(a)} Substituting $s = 1-m$:
    \[
      u = 1 - (1-m)^2 - m^2 = -2m^2 + 2m.
    \]
    First-order condition: $-4m + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow m^* = \tfrac{1}{2}$.

    \textbf{(b.i)}
    \[
      U = 1 - (1-m)^2 - 3m^2 = -4m^2 + 2m.
    \]
    F.O.C.: $m_c = \tfrac{1}{4}$. Lucas plays more music than the community
    prefers because he does not internalise the negative noise externality
    he imposes ($U = u - 2m^2$, where $-2m^2$ is the community's noise
    disutility).

    \textbf{(b.ii)} $(s_c, m_c) = (\tfrac{3}{4}, \tfrac{1}{4})$ is Pareto
    efficient. All $m \in [\tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{2}]$ are Pareto
    efficient: for $m < \tfrac{1}{4}$ both utilities increase if $m$
    rises; for $m > \tfrac{1}{2}$ both decrease if $m$ rises; in between,
    one utility rises and the other falls.

    \textbf{(c)}
    \[
      W = u + U = -6m^2 + 4m.
    \]
    F.O.C.: $m_s = \tfrac{1}{3}$, $s_s = \tfrac{2}{3}$.

    \textbf{(d)} Two possible mechanisms:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item \textbf{Pigouvian tax}: charge Lucas a tax of $t$ per unit of
            music played (e.g.\ on $m - \tfrac{1}{4}$ when $m \ge
            \tfrac{1}{4}$). This raises the marginal private cost of music,
            internalising the noise externality.
      \item \textbf{Silence subsidy}: pay Lucas $\sigma$ per unit of silence
            (e.g.\ on $s > \tfrac{3}{4}$). This increases the marginal
            private benefit of silence, making music relatively less
            attractive.
    \end{itemize}

title: Leveraging the Situation
statement:
Consider two firms in different competitive markets, both facing the
    same price $P$. There is no entry or exit. Firm~A has fixed cost $FC_A$
    and marginal cost $MC_A(q) = 2 + q$. Firm~B has no fixed costs and the
    same marginal cost $MC_B(q) = 2 + q$.

    \begin{enumerate}[(a)]
      \item (20 points) For any given price $P$, determine the
            profit-maximising output for each firm.
            \textit{Hint: variable cost equals the area of the trapezoid
            under the marginal cost curve up to the optimal quantity.}

      \item (20 points) Suppose price doubles from $P = 6$ to $P' = 12$.
            Compute the absolute change in operating profit for Firm~A and
            Firm~B. Express answers as functions of $FC_A$ only.

      \item (15 points) Define the degree of operating leverage as
            \[
              \text{OL} = \frac{\%\,\Delta\,\text{operating profit}}
                               {\%\,\Delta\,\text{revenue}}.
            \]
            Calculate OL for both firms as price rises from $P=6$ to
            $P'=12$, and explain the implications.

      \item (25 points)
            \begin{enumerate}[(i)]
              \item Explain how operating leverage relates to variability of
                    a firm's profit.
              \item Name and briefly explain two other financial risks that
                    affect profit variability.
            \end{enumerate}

      \item (20 points) Provide two sectors with typically \textbf{high}
            operating leverage and two with \textbf{low} operating leverage,
            briefly justifying each.
    \end{enumerate}
solution:
\textbf{(a)} Variable cost: $VC(q) = 2q + q^2/2$. Shutdown condition:
    $P \ge AVC_{\min} = 2$. Profit-maximising output (if $P \ge 2$):
    $q^* = P - 2$.

    \textbf{(b)} General profit: $\Pi = (P-2)^2/2 - FC$.
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Firm A: $\Pi_A^t = 8 - FC_A$, $\Pi_A^{t+1} = 50 - FC_A$,
            $\Delta\Pi_A = 42$.
      \item Firm B: $\Pi_B^t = 8$, $\Pi_B^{t+1} = 50$,
            $\Delta\Pi_B = 42$.
    \end{itemize}
    Both have the same absolute change because $FC_A$ cancels.

    \textbf{(c)} Revenue at $P=6$: 24; at $P'=12$: 120.
    $\%\Delta R = 400\%$ for both.
    \[
      OL_A = \frac{42/(8-FC_A)}{4} = \frac{21}{16-2FC_A}, \qquad
      OL_B = \frac{42/8}{4} = \frac{21}{16} \approx 1.31.
    \]
    Since $FC_A > 0$ reduces the base profit $\Pi_A^t$, $OL_A > OL_B$:
    fixed costs amplify the sensitivity of profits to revenue changes.

    \textbf{(d.i)} Higher operating leverage amplifies both positive and
    negative revenue shocks into larger percentage profit swings, increasing
    earnings volatility.

    \textbf{(d.ii)} Financial leverage (fixed interest obligations amplify
    operating profit fluctuations into larger net-income swings) and
    foreign-exchange exposure (currency movements affect revenues or input
    costs independently of operations).

    \textbf{(e)} \textbf{High OL}: airlines (aircraft, infrastructure) and
    utilities (power plants, networks). \textbf{Low OL}: consulting (mainly
    variable labour) and staffing agencies (costs scale with placements).

title: The Visible Hand
subtopic: Coordination Games / Fiscal Policy / Big Push
statement:
Investment plays a crucial role in driving economic growth, but it is
    also the most volatile component of GDP, as illustrated in Figure~1.
    Unlike consumption or government spending, investment decisions often
    depend on expectations about what others will do, leading to strong
    complementarities and social interdependence.

    \begin{center}
    \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{../images/q0031_fig1.png}
    \end{center}

    Consider an economy with 250 identical private firms. Each firm~$i$
    simultaneously decides whether to invest ($I_i = 1$) or not invest
    ($I_i = 0$). Investing requires a private cost of~1. If a firm invests,
    it earns revenue
    \[
      R = \frac{2(P + G)}{250},
    \]
    where $P = \sum_{i=1}^{250} I_i$ is total private investment and $G$ is
    the government's public investment. A non-investing firm retains a
    current profit of~1.

    \begin{enumerate}[(a)]
      \item (25 points) In national accounts, what is included in
            ``Investment''? Give a concise definition and list the main
            categories.

      \item (25 points) Some theorists argue government spending can ``crowd
            out'' private investment. Briefly describe the mechanism.

      \item (25 points) Suppose $G = 0$.
            \begin{enumerate}[(i)]
              \item Show that \emph{all firms invest} ($I_i=1$ for all $i$)
                    is a Nash equilibrium.
              \item Show that \emph{no firm invests} ($I_i=0$ for all $i$)
                    is also a Nash equilibrium.
            \end{enumerate}

      \item (25 points) Now the government sets $G = 200$, financed by a
            lump-sum tax of \$0.80 on each firm.
            \begin{enumerate}[(i)]
              \item Show that \emph{all firms invest} is now the \textbf{unique}
                    Nash equilibrium.
              \item Interpret these results: what do they imply about
                    multiple equilibria, coordination failures, and the logic
                    of a ``big push''? Provide a brief policy implication.
            \end{enumerate}
    \end{enumerate}
solution:
\textbf{(a)} Investment (gross fixed capital formation plus inventory
    changes) covers: business machinery and equipment, non-residential
    structures, residential construction, intellectual property products
    (R\&D, software), and changes in inventories. Financial asset purchases
    are excluded.

    \textbf{(b)} Higher government borrowing absorbs scarce loanable funds,
    pushing up real interest rates and making private investment more
    expensive --- the classical crowding-out mechanism. Alternatively,
    higher taxes reduce after-tax returns to private investment.

    \textbf{(c.i)} If all firms invest, $P=250$ and the payoff to investing is
    $1 + 2(250)/250 - 1 = 2 > 1$ (payoff from not investing). No firm
    deviates.

    \textbf{(c.ii)} If no firm invests, $P=0$ and the payoff to a single
    deviating firm is $1 + 2(1)/250 - 1 = 2/250 < 1$. No firm deviates.

    \textbf{(d.i)} With $G=200$ and tax 0.8 per firm. Even if $k=0$ other
    firms invest, the payoff to investing is $0.2 + 2(201)/250 - 1 = 0.808 >
    0.2$ (payoff from not investing). Investing is a dominant strategy, so
    all-invest is the unique equilibrium.

    \textbf{(d.ii)} Without public investment the economy has two equilibria:
    a good one (all invest) and a bad one (no one invests). This is a
    coordination failure: even if the high-investment outcome is efficient,
    pessimistic expectations can trap the economy in the low-investment
    equilibrium. A sufficiently large public investment (``big push'')
    eliminates the bad equilibrium by making investment a dominant strategy,
    justifying temporary fiscal stimulus to coordinate private expectations.

title: So where is this discount factor, anyway?
statement:
Asset pricing theory provides a framework for determining the fair value
    of financial assets based on expected cash flows and associated risks.

    \begin{enumerate}[(a)]
      \item Consider a risk-free asset paying cash flow $C$ at $t+1$, priced
            today as $P_t = C/(1+r)$. Explain the intuition behind this
            present value formula. Why are future cash flows discounted, and
            what does the discount rate represent?

      \item When investing in stocks, what are the two main sources of return?

      \item The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) gives the required return
            on stock~$i$ as
            \[
              R_i^e = R_f + \beta_i(R_m - R_f).
            \]
            What does $(R_m - R_f)$ represent? Why should stocks with higher
            $\beta$ require higher expected returns?

      \item The Fama--French three-factor model extends CAPM:
            \[
              R_i^e = R_f + \beta_i(R_m - R_f) + s_i \cdot SMB + h_i \cdot HML,
            \]
            where $SMB > 0$ and $HML > 0$ empirically. Formulate one
            hypothesis explaining why small firms outperform large firms, and
            one hypothesis explaining why value firms outperform growth firms.
            \textit{Hint: consider how value and growth firms perform during
            economic downturns.}
    \end{enumerate}
solution:
\textbf{(a)} Money has time value: a dollar today can be invested to
    earn returns, so future cash flows must be discounted to reflect the
    opportunity cost of waiting. The discount rate $r$ represents the
    required rate of return --- the compensation investors demand for
    deferring consumption. For risk-free assets this equals the risk-free
    rate.

    \textbf{(b)} Dividends (periodic cash distributions) and capital gains
    (appreciation in share price realised upon sale).

    \textbf{(c)} $(R_m - R_f)$ is the \emph{market risk premium}: the extra
    return investors demand for bearing systematic market risk. $\beta_i$
    measures a stock's exposure to systematic risk; since investors are
    risk-averse, higher-beta stocks must offer higher expected returns to
    compensate for greater risk.

    \textbf{(d)} \emph{Size premium}: small firms face greater credit,
    liquidity, and operational risks than large firms, requiring higher
    returns as compensation. \emph{Value premium}: value firms have
    short-duration cash flows concentrated in the near term; in recessions
    these near-term cash flows deteriorate sharply, exposing value investors
    to higher systematic (cyclical) risk. Investors demand a premium for
    bearing this downturn risk.

title: Drawing the Line
subtopic: Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium / Integer Game
statement:
This question consists of two independent games that share underlying
    similarities. Read the full question before answering.

    \medskip
    \textbf{Integer Game.} Three economists simultaneously choose a positive
    integer.
    \begin{itemize}
      \item The player who chose the \emph{smallest unique} number wins.
      \item If all three choose the same number, the winner is chosen
            uniformly at random.
    \end{itemize}
    \textit{Examples}: $(1,2,3) \Rightarrow$ player with~1 wins;
    $(2,2,3) \Rightarrow$ player with~3 wins;
    $(3,3,3) \Rightarrow$ uniform random winner.

    \begin{enumerate}[(a)]
      \item (20 points)
            \begin{enumerate}[(i)]
              \item Give an example of a non-symmetric pure-strategy Nash
                    equilibrium and briefly justify why it is an equilibrium.
              \item Show that the strategy profile where each player
                    independently chooses uniformly from $\{1,2,3\}$ is
                    \emph{not} a Nash equilibrium.
            \end{enumerate}

      \item (35 points) Assume a symmetric mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium
            exists in which each player uses distribution $(p_1, p_2, p_3,
            \ldots)$ over the positive integers.
            \begin{enumerate}[(i)]
              \item Prove that the sequence $(p_n)$ is non-increasing.
              \item Prove that every $p_n > 0$.
            \end{enumerate}

      \item (20 points) \textbf{Negotiation Game.} Three firms play a
            repeated game. Each round, each firm simultaneously chooses
            \emph{Cooperate} or \emph{Defect}.
            \begin{itemize}
              \item All cooperate $\Rightarrow$ no deal, game continues.
              \item Exactly one defects $\Rightarrow$ that firm wins, game ends.
              \item Exactly two defect $\Rightarrow$ the cooperator wins, game ends.
              \item All defect $\Rightarrow$ uniform random winner, game ends.
            \end{itemize}
            \begin{enumerate}[(i)]
              \item Under any symmetric strategy, what is each firm's
                    expected payoff?
              \item If each firm defects with probability $p$, find the
                    value of $p$ that defines a symmetric mixed-strategy
                    Nash equilibrium.
            \end{enumerate}

      \item (25 points) Using the result from (c), determine the symmetric
            mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium of the Integer Game.
    \end{enumerate}
solution:
\textbf{(a.i)} $(1, 2, 2)$ is a non-symmetric pure-strategy NE. The
    player with~1 wins for sure and has no incentive to deviate; the
    players with~2 cannot improve by deviating (any other choice either
    still loses or ties in the random draw).

    \textbf{(a.ii)} Under uniform play on $\{1,2,3\}$, expected payoff is
    $1/3$. Deviating to always play~1: wins whenever neither opponent plays
    1 (probability $4/9$) plus $1/3$ chance when all play~1 ($1/9$).
    Payoff $= 4/9 + 1/27 = 13/27 > 1/3$. Profitable deviation $\Rightarrow$
    not a NE.

    \textbf{(b.i)} In a symmetric NE the winning probability is maximised
    by placing mass on smaller numbers. If $p_m > p_n$ for some $m > n$,
    swapping those probabilities increases the chance of holding the
    smallest unique number without changing payoffs in tie scenarios.
    Hence $(p_n)$ must be non-increasing.

    \textbf{(b.ii)} Suppose there is a smallest $n$ with $p_n = 0$ (and
    $n > 1$, since $p_1 = 0$ gives a trivially degenerate equilibrium).
    Redistributing half of $p_{n-1}$ to $p_n$ strictly increases expected
    payoff (new winning cases open up without losing existing ones),
    contradicting equilibrium. Hence $p_n > 0$ for all $n$.

    \textbf{(c.i)} By symmetry each firm wins with probability $1/3$,
    so expected payoff is $\boxed{1/3}$.

    \textbf{(c.ii)} Swapping ``Cooperate'' and ``Defect'' leaves the game
    structurally identical, so in a symmetric NE $p = 1-p$, giving
    $p = 1/2$.

    \textbf{(d)} The Integer Game and the Negotiation Game are structurally
    equivalent: choosing integer~$n$ corresponds to planning to defect in
    round~$n$ if no one defected earlier. Under the $p = 1/2$ equilibrium
    of the Negotiation Game, the probability of first defecting in round~$n$
    is $(1/2)^n$. Hence the unique symmetric mixed-strategy NE of the
    Integer Game is:
    \[
      p_n = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n, \quad \forall\, n \in \mathbb{N}.
    \]

title: OUR Street
statement:
A local community is deciding whether to fund a street lighting system.
    Once installed, the lighting benefits all residents equally. Each resident
    prefers having street lights to not having them, but does not value them
    enough to pay for the entire cost individually. Which of the following best
    explains why a purely voluntary market is likely to underprovide street
    lighting?
choices:
Because street lighting has increasing marginal costs, private firms
          can't supply it efficiently.

Because individuals don't consider the benefits that street lighting
          provides to others when deciding how much to contribute.

Because street lighting is non-rival but excludable, firms can't
          charge an efficient price.

Because lights are an inferior good and therefore have little
          perceived value to consumers.
solution:
Street lighting is a non-rival, non-excludable public good. Individuals
    have incentives to free-ride, ignoring the positive externalities their
    contributions create for others, leading to underprovision. (B) is correct.

title: The Coupon That Hits Home
statement:
DeltaCorp is issuing 10-year bonds with a face value of \$1{,}000. The
    current market yield for bonds with similar risk and maturity is 8\% per
    annum. The company wants the bonds to trade at par value. What annual
    coupon rate must DeltaCorp set?
choices: 6\% per annum.
7\% per annum.
8\% per annum.
10\% per annum.
solution:
A bond trades at par when its coupon rate equals the market yield. At an
    8\% coupon rate, the present value of all cash flows equals the \$1{,}000
    face value. (C) is correct.

title: Follow the Smell(s)
statement:
Darko earned a budget of \$16 and chooses between cotton candy ($c$, price
    \$1) and popcorn ($p$, price \$4). His utility function is
    $u(c, p) = \sqrt{c} + 2\sqrt{p}$. Which bundle will Darko choose?
solution:
We evaluate feasible bundles directly. Option~(A): cost $= 0 + 16 = \$16$,
    $U = 0 + 2\sqrt{4} = 4$. Option~(B): cost $= 2 + 24 = \$26$ --- infeasible.
    Option~(C): cost $= 8 + 8 = \$16$, $U = \sqrt{8} + 2\sqrt{2} \approx 5.66$.
    Option~(D): cost $= 12 + 4 = \$16$,
    $U = \sqrt{12} + 2\sqrt{1} \approx 5.46$.
    Bundle~(C) satisfies the budget constraint and maximises utility.
    \textbf{(C)} is correct.

title: No Food, No Fun
statement:
Braeconomia is a major agricultural-exporting country responsible for 82\%
    of the galaxy's food exports (40\% of galaxy's GDP). In December 2030, it
    ceased all exports for 6 months. What was the primary economic effect on
    interplanetary markets?
choices:
Importing nations immediately began producing their own food, given
          variable capital.

The interplanetary market suffered a major supply shock, increasing
          price levels and unemployment.

The interplanetary market suffered a major demand shock, decreasing
          price levels and unemployment.
There was no significant effect on interplanetary markets.
solution:
A sudden halt in 82\% of food supply constitutes a large negative supply
    shock: less output is available at any given price level, causing prices to
    rise and production to fall, resulting in stagflation. (B) is correct.

title: Same Notes, Different Song
statement:
Obeconomia has three age groups: Youth (50M), Adults (30M), Elderly (20M)
    with unemployment rates 10\%, 3\%, 7\% respectively, giving an overall
    unemployment rate of 7.3\%. After demographic shifts to Youth (20M),
    Adults (50M), Elderly (30M) with unchanged group-specific rates, what
    happens to overall unemployment?
choices: Overall unemployment rises because within-group rates rise.
Overall unemployment stays constant at 7.3\%.

Overall unemployment falls from 7.3\% to 5.6\% due to composition
          effects.

Overall unemployment increases from 5.6\% to 7.3\% due to
          composition effects.
solution:
The new overall unemployment rate is:
    \[
      \frac{0.10 \times 20 + 0.03 \times 50 + 0.07 \times 30}{100}
      = \frac{2 + 1.5 + 2.1}{100} = 5.6\%.
    \]
    The shift toward the lower-unemployment adult group reduces aggregate
    unemployment despite unchanged group-specific rates --- a pure composition
    effect. (C) is correct.

title: Shielding from the Lion
statement:
QuietFork Foods compares two financing options with identical operating
    profit. Option~A uses all equity; Option~B uses a mix of debt and equity.
    Both face corporate income tax. What is the primary tax advantage of
    Option~B?
choices: Interest expense reduces taxable income, lowering total taxes paid.
Debt financing eliminates the need to pay taxes entirely.
Operating profit is higher with debt financing.
Equity holders receive tax deductions on dividends.
solution:
Interest payments on debt are tax-deductible before calculating corporate
    taxes, whereas dividends are paid from after-tax income. This debt tax
    shield (Interest $\times$ Tax Rate) gives Option~B a lower effective tax
    burden, increasing after-tax value available to all capital providers.
    (A) is correct.

title: One Project to Rule Them All
statement:
ObeCorp evaluates four mutually exclusive projects with similar risk.
    Alpha: NPV = \$15M, IRR = 18\%, Payback = 4~yr.
    Beta: NPV = \$12M, IRR = 22\%, Payback = 2~yr.
    Gamma: NPV = \$10M, IRR = 30\%, Payback = 1.5~yr.
    Delta: NPV = \$8M, IRR = 25\%, Payback = 1~yr.
    The cost of capital is 12\%. Which project should ObeCorp select?
choices: Project Alpha, because it has the highest NPV.
Project Beta, because it balances IRR and payback effectively.
Project Gamma, because it has the highest IRR.
Project Delta, because it has the shortest payback period.
solution:
NPV is the correct criterion for mutually exclusive projects: it measures
    absolute value creation, accounts for the time value of money at the
    appropriate discount rate, and is consistent with shareholder wealth
    maximisation. Project Alpha's \$15M NPV exceeds all alternatives. (A) is
    correct.

title: Diversify or Die Trying
statement:
Ricardo holds an equity portfolio highly correlated with the stock market.
    He evaluates two funds: Fund Equity (high returns, invests in stocks) and
    Fund Bond (moderate returns, invests in bonds). Which should he choose to
    best diversify his portfolio?
choices: Equity Fund, since it offers higher returns with low systematic risk.

Equity Fund, because additional equity exposure won't significantly
          increase overall risk.
Bond Fund, as it provides diversification and high yields.

Bond Fund, because it offers diversification together with moderate
          returns.
solution:
Ricardo's portfolio is already heavily concentrated in equities. Fund Bond
    provides diversification through low (or negative) correlation with stocks.
    Modern portfolio theory shows that combining asset classes with low
    correlations reduces overall portfolio risk while maintaining acceptable
    returns. (D) is correct.

title: Keynesia
statement:
Keynesia has sticky prices and wages. The consumption function is
    $C = 10 + 0.8Y$. A fiscal stimulus increases investment by 10 billion.
    Find the Keynesian multiplier $m$ and the resulting change in output
    $\Delta Y$.
choices: $m = 0.2$ and $\Delta Y = 2$.
$m = 0.8$ and $\Delta Y = 8$.
$m = 2$ and $\Delta Y = 20$.
$m = 5$ and $\Delta Y = 50$.
solution:
The Keynesian multiplier is:
    \[
      m = \frac{1}{1 - MPC} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5.
    \]
    With $\Delta I = 10$ billion:
    \[
      \Delta Y = m \times \Delta I = 5 \times 10 = 50 \text{ billion.}
    \]
    (D) is correct.

title: Bubble Tea or Artisanal Coffee?
choices: Both methods show identical inflation.

The L-Index shows higher inflation than the P-Index due to
          substitution bias.

The P-Index shows higher inflation than the L-Index due to
          substitution bias.

The L-Index shows lower inflation than the P-Index because it uses
          outdated weights.
solution:
The Laspeyres index overweights goods that have become relatively more
    expensive (artisanal coffee), while rational consumers substitute toward
    cheaper alternatives (bubble tea). This substitution bias causes
    $L \geq P$ mathematically. The fixed-basket L-Index therefore overstates
    inflation compared to the annually-updated P-Index. (B) is correct.

title: Fed Facts
statement:
Consider four statements about monetary policy and central banks. Which are
    correct?\\[4pt]
    \textbf{I.}~Central banks use reserve requirements as the primary
    inflation-control tool, increasing ratios during expansions.\\
    \textbf{II.}~Quantitative easing involves large-scale purchases of
    government or corporate securities when conventional policy fails at the
    zero lower bound.\\
    \textbf{III.}~To lower interest rates, central banks purchase government
    bonds in open market operations, injecting liquidity.\\
    \textbf{IV.}~The Federal Reserve has a dual mandate (price stability and
    maximum employment); the Central Bank of Brazil has a single mandate
    (inflation targeting).
choices: Only I and III.
Only I and IV.
Only II, III and IV.
Only II and III.
solution:
Statement~I is false: policy interest rates (not reserve requirements) are
    the primary modern tool for controlling inflation. Statements~II, III,
    and~IV are all accurate descriptions of modern monetary policy practice.
    (C) is correct.

title: Combining Forces
statement:
NPV profiles for Projects~F and~G show the following: Project~F has a
    positive NPV at all discount rates (NPV declines as the rate rises).
    Project~G starts positive at a 0\% discount rate, crosses zero NPV at
    10\%, and becomes negative thereafter. At a 15\% discount rate,
    NPV(F) = +\$10M and NPV(G) = $-$\$10M. What can be concluded?
choices:
Only Project~F should be undertaken at any cost of capital below
          20\%.

Both projects should be undertaken if the cost of capital is below
          15\%.

If the projects were mutually exclusive, Project~G adoption would
          depend on the cost of capital.
The joint realisation of both projects has an IRR of 15\%.
solution:
At a 15\% discount rate: NPV(F) = +\$10M and NPV(G) = $-$\$10M, so
    NPV(F + G) = \$0. This means 15\% is exactly the IRR of the combined
    portfolio --- the rate at which undertaking both projects simultaneously
    yields zero net present value. (D) is correct.

title: When Ceteris Isn't Paribus
statement:
A researcher compares wages: training programme participants earn \$24/hr
    vs.\ non-participants at \$18/hr, concluding the programme adds \$6/hr.
    However, 60\% of participants hold high school diplomas compared to 20\%
    of non-participants, and a diploma independently adds \$5/hr to wages.
    What is the actual programme effect on wages?
choices: It is not possible to determine the true value.
solution:
Controlling for diploma composition: among participants without a diploma
    (40\%), expected wage $= 24 - 0.6 \times 5 = \$21$/hr. Among
    non-participants without a diploma (80\%), expected wage
    $= 18 - 0.2 \times 5 = \$17$/hr. The pure training effect is
    $21 - 17 = \$4$/hr. The remaining \$2 of the raw \$6 gap is attributable
    to selection bias (diploma endowment). (B) is correct.

title: ZaneTech
statement:
ZaneTech evaluates a project requiring a \$50M initial investment. The
    project generates a Year~1 cash flow of \$6.8M, growing at 3\% annually
    for 10~years (the project terminates at Year~10). The discount rate is
    10\%. Should ZaneTech proceed with the project?
choices: Yes, NPV is positive at approximately \$10.5 million.
Yes, NPV is positive at approximately \$5.3 million.
No, NPV is negative at approximately $-$\$4.2 million.
No, NPV is negative at approximately $-$\$3.2 million.
solution:
Using the growing perpetuity (Gordon Growth) approach to value a growing
    annuity:
    \[
      PV_{\text{perp from yr 1}} = \frac{6.8}{0.10 - 0.03} = \frac{6.8}{0.07}
      \approx \$97.14\text{M}.
    \]
    \[
      PV_{\text{perp from yr 11}} = \frac{6.8 \times 1.03^{10}}{0.07 \times
      1.10^{10}} \approx \frac{9.14}{0.07 \times 2.594} \approx \$50.34\text{M}.
    \]
    \[
      NPV = -50 + 97.14 - 50.34 \approx -\$3.20\text{M}.
    \]
    The NPV is negative, so ZaneTech should not proceed. (D) is correct.

title: Playing It Safe
statement:
William, a conservative investor, evaluates four investment options in
    Rivelli Corporation: ordinary shares, preferred shares, subordinated
    debentures, and stock options. Which presents the best choice for
    minimising investment risk?
choices: Ordinary shares.
Preferred shares.
Subordinated debentures.
Stock options.
solution:
Capital structure priority from highest to lowest creditor protection:
    senior secured debt $>$ senior unsecured debt $>$ subordinated debt $>$
    preferred shares $>$ ordinary shares. Subordinated debentures offer fixed
    contractual interest payments (a legal obligation), priority over all
    equity holders in liquidation, and creditor status --- providing superior
    protection compared to any equity instrument. Stock options carry the
    highest risk. (C) is correct.

title: Do I Take the Bus or the Subway?
statement:
Eduarda's Marshallian demand functions are:
    \[
      B = \frac{2m}{2p_B + p_S}, \qquad S = \frac{m}{2p_B + p_S},
    \]
    where $B$ denotes bus rides, $S$ subway trips, $m$ income, $p_B$ the price
    of bus rides, and $p_S$ the price of subway trips. What type of preferences
    does this demand system represent?
choices: Perfect substitutes: $u(B,S) = \alpha B + \beta S$.
Perfect complements: $u(B,S) = \min\{\alpha B,\, \beta S\}$.
Cobb-Douglas: $u(B,S) = B^{\alpha} S^{\beta}$.
Quasi-linear: $u(B,S) = \alpha B + \beta \ln S$.
solution:
The demand ratio $B/S = 2$ is constant regardless of prices or income ---
    the defining characteristic of perfect complements. This is consistent with
    $u(B,S) = \min\{B,\, 2S\}$: Eduarda always requires exactly two bus rides
    per subway trip. The demands are therefore not driven by substitution but
    by a fixed consumption ratio. (B) is correct.

title: Who ate my cake?
statement:
Argentiland has 50\% monthly inflation; Brazilkistan has 3\% annual
    inflation. In Argentiland, companies keep product sizes stable but raise
    prices regularly. In Brazilkistan, companies reduce product sizes while
    keeping nominal prices stable (shrinkflation). What best explains this
    difference in firm behaviour?
choices: Brazilkistan consumers are less likely to accept price increases.

Argentiland consumers anticipate frequent price increases, so
          companies can raise prices without changing product sizes.
Argentiland companies face higher production costs.

Higher inflation makes Argentiland companies prefer price increases
          to shrinkflation.
solution:
In high-inflation environments like Argentiland, price increases are
    anticipated and normalised, greatly reducing consumer resistance to nominal
    price changes. In moderate-inflation economies like Brazilkistan, consumers
    are far more sensitive to visible price increases, making shrinkflation --- a
    covert real price increase --- the preferred strategy for maintaining revenues
    without triggering consumer backlash. (B) is correct.

title: Nobody expects the Monetary Inquisition!
statement:
A flexible-price economy expected a 20\% money supply increase; the actual
    increase was 40\%. According to New Classical theory with rational
    expectations, what happens to the price level and to output in the short
    run?
choices:
Both price level and output increase. Had agents expected zero
          increase, the output effect would be larger.

Policy has no effect on output, only on the price level, since agents
          already expected expansion.
Price level and output increase more than under zero expectations.

Both rise; sticky prices cause the full 40\% increase to raise output
          gradually.
solution:
Under New Classical rational expectations, only the \emph{unexpected}
    component of policy affects real output. Here the unexpected component is
    $40\% - 20\% = 20\%$, which raises aggregate demand and output in the
    short run alongside prices. Had agents expected 0\%, the entire 40\%
    expansion would be a surprise, producing a larger real output effect.
    Note that flexible prices rule out sticky-price mechanisms. (A) is correct.

title: Timing Is (Almost) Everything
statement:
A mining company generates continuous cash flows throughout each year.
    Analyst~A argues for the year-end convention (discounting all cash flows
    as if received at year-end); Analyst~B argues for the mid-year convention
    (discounting as if received at mid-year). Which approach is more accurate
    for valuation?
choices:
Analyst~A; year-end convention is standard practice regardless of
          actual cash flow timing.

Analyst~B; mid-year convention better reflects continuous cash flow
          timing.

Analyst~B; even though cash should be modelled as arriving at the
          beginning of each period.

Both approaches are equally accurate as they discount the same cash
          flows.
solution:
For evenly distributed continuous cash flows, the average timing is
    mid-year (month~6), not year-end (month~12). Using the year-end convention
    systematically undervalues the asset by assuming cash arrives on average
    6~months later than it actually does. The mid-year convention corrects
    this bias and produces more accurate present values. (B) is correct.

title: Is this (Deadweight) Loss?
statement:
A monopolist faces a linear, downward-sloping demand curve and operates
    with constant marginal cost. A technology shock lowers the monopolist's
    marginal cost. What happens to deadweight loss?
choices:
Deadweight loss increases because the socially optimal quantity
          increases more than the monopoly quantity.

Deadweight loss decreases because firm production approaches the
          social optimum.

Deadweight loss becomes zero because the firm produces the social
          optimum.

Deadweight loss does not change because the fraction of efficient
